name: Russell's Paradox
guide_hints: In this level, we will prove that there is no 'set of all sets', also known as Russell's paradox.
The goal is to prove that there does not exist a set A such that for all B, B belongs to A. We will write this as `¬¨(‚ąÉA(‚ąÄB(B‚ąąA)))`.
First, we will assume the negation of our goal using the contra rule. Type `mission cons1 contra goal`. This assumes `‚ąÉA(‚ąÄB(B‚ąąA))` (let's call the premise ant1) and changes our goal to ‚ä•.

Next, we want to instantiate this universal set A. However, `ExElim` is not directly provided in this level, so we must rely on other tactics if needed, or we can use `intro ant1 U` which introduces a universal set `U` such that `‚ąÄB(B‚ąąU)`.
Now we use the Axiom of Specification to define a set R of all sets that do not contain themselves.
Since the set builder notation macro can be tricky with operator precedence in formulas, we will define it explicitly. Type `cf thm1 ‚ąÉR ‚ąÄx (x‚ąąR ‚áĒ (x‚ąąU ‚ąß ¬¨(x‚ąąx)))`.
Using the specification axiom, we know such a set exists. Type `apply thm1 specification` to prove it.
Next, we can instantiate R from thm1 (which is an existential formula). Once instantiated, we can substitute `x` with `R`.
This leads to `R‚ąąR ‚áĒ (R‚ąąU ‚ąß ¬¨(R‚ąąR))`. Since we know `R‚ąąU` (because `U` contains all sets), this simplifies to `R‚ąąR ‚áĒ ¬¨(R‚ąąR)` which is a contradiction (‚ä•).
Use your propositional logic tactics (`imply`, `and`, `left`, `right`) and standard rules to derive ‚ä• and close the mission!
